module: Immunocompromised patients Part IV: Preventing Infection in the immunocompromised patient

sections.Hand Hygiene: The most important intervention for preventing ICU infection is hand hygiene. Alcohol-based hand rubs combined with chlorhexidine cover Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria, viruses, mycobacteria, and fungi — but are INEFFECTIVE against C. difficile and other spore-forming organisms (soap and water required for these). WHO SAVE LIVES identifies 5 moments: (1) before touching a patient, (2) before clean/aseptic procedures, (3) after body fluid exposure/risk, (4) after touching a patient, (5) after touching patient surroundings. Video surveillance data show healthcare workers have worse compliance than patients' relatives.
sections.Isolation: Neutropenic patients (non-HSCT) do NOT require isolation rooms or specific barrier precautions — standard precautions suffice. HSCT patients require a protective environment: single well-sealed rooms with >12 air exchanges per hour and HEPA filtration to reduce invasive Aspergillosis risk. Isolation is primarily discussed for contagious patients to reduce cross-infection; in absence of adequate isolation rooms, barrier precautions (gloves, gown) + good hand hygiene are paramount.
sections.Standards for ICU Cleaning and Design: Hydrogen peroxide vapor decontamination is superior to conventional cleaning but can only be used in enclosed rooms (toxic to humans). UV radiation devices enhance clearance of MDROs from the environment but are LESS effective against C. difficile and other spore-forming organisms. Standard non-ionic surfactant solution (1:500 dilution) may NOT completely clear MRSA.
sections.Preventing ICU Device-Related Infections: VAP bundles include: subglottic suctioning ETTs, head-of-bed elevation to 45°, antimicrobial-coated ETTs, routine oral care, SOD/SDD, daily weaning trials, early tracheostomy. SOD = topical non-absorbable antimicrobials to oropharynx only. SDD = oral non-absorbable antimicrobials + short-course systemic antibiotic. Both reduce VAP and modestly reduce ICU mortality. Longitudinal ICU follow-up has NOT shown increased resistant organisms with SDD adoption. In cancer patients for long-term CVC: subcutaneous implanted ports have lowest CLABSI risk vs tunneled vs non-tunneled catheters. Antibiotic-coated catheters and antimicrobial lock solutions reduce CLABSI and may be used when removal not feasible.
sections.Antimicrobial Prophylaxis: HIV prophylaxis by CD4 count: PCP (TMP-SMX) at CD4 <200/µL; Toxoplasmosis (TMP-SMX) at CD4 <100/µL; Histoplasmosis (itraconazole) at CD4 <100/µL in endemic areas; MAC (azithromycin or clarithromycin) at CD4 <50/µL; TB (isoniazid + pyridoxine) at any CD4 if TST >5mm or TB contact. Post-transplant PJP (TMP-SMX): ~12 months for solid organ (longer for lung) vs 6 months for HSCT (longer only if continued immunosuppression). CMV: valganciclovir/ganciclovir — universal prophylaxis (all high-risk patients started regardless of viral load) vs preemptive strategy (monitor viral load, start at threshold before clinical disease). VZV/HSV: acyclovir for seropositive patients not on CMV prophylaxis. Pre-engraftment HSCT: fluoroquinolones reduce Gram-negative infection risk but provide NO mortality benefit; use controversial.
sections.Routine Microbiological Surveillance in: Routine surveillance cultures of patients and environment generate MDRO profiles and enable timely infection control measures. Positive surveillance culture = colonisation, NOT necessarily active infection. Adding targeted decolonisation to surveillance + barrier precautions + hand hygiene further reduces MDRO transmission. Evidence for cost-effectiveness is mixed, but a MDRO outbreak greatly outweighs the cost of surveillance.
sections.Nutrition and Probiotics: Enteral nutrition (EN) preferred over parenteral (PN): preserves gut mucosal integrity and immune function, fewer infectious and GI complications, lower cost. Initiation within 24-48h; permissive underfeeding is well tolerated. Probiotics have modest evidence for C. difficile-associated diarrhoea prevention; evidence for nosocomial pneumonia prevention is mixed. In immunocompromised patients, probiotics remain controversial (safety concerns) though some liver transplant data is favourable. HSCT pre-engraftment: avoid raw/undercooked meals, fresh fruits/vegetables, unpasteurised milk.

questions.question: A 67-year-old mechanically ventilated patient develops profuse watery diarrhoea on day 8 of ICU admission following broad-spectrum antibiotics. Clostridium difficile infection is suspected. The bedside nurse is about to perform morning care. Which hand hygiene method is most appropriate before entering the patient's room?
options: A: Handwashing with antibacterial soap and water
B: Chlorhexidine gluconate solution applied without rinsing
C: Alcohol-based hand rub (ABHR) with ≥60% ethanol
D: Combined chlorhexidine-alcohol preparation
E: No additional precautions beyond standard ABHR used elsewhere on the unit
questions.explanation: Alcohol-based preparations — including combined chlorhexidine-alcohol — are ineffective against C. difficile and other spore-forming organisms. Physical removal with soap and water is required to dislodge spores. The combined chlorhexidine-alcohol preparation is the common near-miss: it is excellent for most ICU pathogens (Gram-positive, Gram-negative, viruses, mycobacteria, fungi) and is generally ideal, but its alcohol component makes it insufficient for spore-formers — broad-spectrum coverage does not extend to spores.
questions.topic: hand hygiene C difficile spores alcohol ineffective soap water

questions.question: A 38-year-old patient undergoes allogeneic haematopoietic stem cell transplantation (HSCT) for acute myeloid leukaemia and is admitted to the ICU post-procedure. Which specific environmental measure is most critical for this patient to reduce the risk of invasive Aspergillosis?
options: A: Standard neutropenia precautions, without requiring any dedicated isolation room at all
B: Droplet precautions in any available side room
C: Contact precautions with gloves and gown alone in a standard side room, without HEPA filtration
D: Reverse barrier nursing with surgical mask and apron only
E: Placement in a single well-sealed room with >12 air exchanges per hour and HEPA filtration
questions.explanation: HSCT patients require a protective environment — single well-sealed rooms with >12 air exchanges per hour and high-efficiency particulate air (HEPA) filtration — specifically to decrease the risk of invasive Aspergillosis. This distinguishes HSCT patients from other neutropenic patients (e.g., those receiving chemotherapy) who do NOT require isolation rooms or specific barrier precautions. Describing standard neutropenia precautions without an isolation room is the key near-miss — the HSCT distinction is a frequent exam discriminator.
questions.topic: HSCT protective environment HEPA air exchanges Aspergillosis isolation

questions.question: An ICU experiences an outbreak of Clostridium difficile infections despite enhanced terminal room cleaning with standard solutions. The infection control team reviews decontamination technologies. Which statement about available methods is most accurate?
options: A: UV radiation devices are equally effective against C. difficile spores and MRSA
B: Hydrogen peroxide vapor is superior to conventional cleaning but requires room enclosure as it is toxic to humans
C: Standard non-ionic surfactant solution diluted 1:500 reliably eradicates MRSA from surfaces
D: Alcohol-based environmental wipes are first-line for C. difficile room terminal decontamination
E: UV radiation devices are consistently more effective than hydrogen peroxide vapor specifically against spore-forming organisms
questions.explanation: Hydrogen peroxide vapor decontamination has been shown superior to conventional cleaning but can only be used in enclosed rooms because it is toxic to humans — limiting its practicality for occupied units. UV radiation devices enhance clearance of MDROs (including MRSA) but are less effective against C. difficile and other spore-forming organisms, so claiming equal or superior UV effectiveness against spores is incorrect. Standard non-ionic surfactant solution (1:500) may not completely clear MRSA. Alcohol-based wipes are ineffective against spores.
questions.section: Standards for ICU Cleaning and Design
questions.topic: H2O2 vapor UV decontamination C difficile MRSA MDR cleaning ICU

questions.question: A quality improvement team audits VAP prevention practices in a mixed ICU. They review which interventions constitute a standard VAP prevention bundle. Which of the following is NOT a recognised component of a standard VAP prevention bundle?
options: A: Head-of-bed elevation to 45 degrees
B: Prophylactic systemic antifungal therapy
C: Routine oral care with chlorhexidine
D: Daily assessment and weaning trials to minimise ventilator duration
E: Subglottic suctioning endotracheal tubes
questions.explanation: Standard VAP bundles include subglottic suctioning ETTs, head-of-bed elevation, oral care, selective decontamination (SOD/SDD), and measures to reduce ventilator duration (daily weaning trials, early tracheostomy). Prophylactic systemic antifungal therapy is NOT a bundle component — antifungal prophylaxis is reserved for specific high-risk immunocompromised populations (prolonged neutropenia, post-transplant) based on the underlying disease, not as universal VAP prevention. Chlorhexidine oral care is the near-miss here since it IS a genuine bundle element; the question tests whether candidates add antifungals inappropriately.
questions.topic: VAP bundle components NOT antifungal prophylaxis subglottic oral care

questions.question: During an ICU governance meeting, a proposal is made to implement selective digestive decontamination (SDD) for all mechanically ventilated patients. A consultant raises concerns about long-term selection for antibiotic-resistant organisms. Which statement best reflects current evidence?
options: A: SDD uses only intravenous systemic antibiotics without any topical oropharyngeal component
B: SDD is formally contraindicated in ICUs with background resistance rates above 10%
C: Longitudinal follow-up of ICUs adopting SDD has not demonstrated increased resistant organism levels
D: SDD has been shown in longitudinal studies to significantly increase multidrug-resistant organism carriage in adopting ICUs
E: SDD reduces VAP rates but has no demonstrated effect on ICU mortality
questions.explanation: Despite theoretical concerns about resistance selection, longitudinal follow-up of ICUs that adopted SDD has not demonstrated increased levels of resistant organisms — the evidence points contrary to the intuitive concern. SDD has also been shown to modestly reduce mortality, not just VAP rates, so describing only a VAP effect understates its benefit. SDD consists of topical oral non-absorbable antimicrobials combined with a short-course systemic antibiotic, not IV antibiotics alone. There is no formal resistance-rate threshold contraindication for SDD.
questions.topic: SDD SOD resistance evidence mortality VAP decontamination digestive

questions.question: A 54-year-old with non-Hodgkin's lymphoma requires long-term venous access for six cycles of chemotherapy. He has no prior central venous access. Which type of venous access device carries the lowest risk of catheter-related bloodstream infection (CLABSI) for long-term use?
options: A: Peripherally inserted central catheter (PICC)
B: Non-tunneled central venous catheter (CVC) inserted at the internal jugular vein
C: Subcutaneously implanted port (port-a-cath)
D: Tunneled central venous catheter (e.g., Hickman line)
E: Antibiotic-coated non-tunneled CVC
questions.explanation: For long-term venous access in cancer patients, subcutaneously implanted ports (port-a-caths) carry the lowest infection risk because the reservoir remains subcutaneous and is only accessed intermittently, minimising sustained skin breaching and biofilm formation. In order of increasing infection risk: implanted port < tunneled catheter < non-tunneled CVC. An antibiotic-coated non-tunneled catheter reduces infection compared to a standard non-tunneled CVC but does not match the risk profile of an implanted port when long-term access is planned.
questions.topic: CLABSI cancer catheter type port tunneled non-tunneled infection risk

questions.question: A 32-year-old HIV-positive man is admitted to the ICU with respiratory failure. His CD4 count is 45 cells/µL. He is already receiving trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole (TMP-SMX) for PCP prophylaxis. Which additional prophylaxis should now be initiated?
options: A: Azithromycin or clarithromycin for Mycobacterium avium complex (MAC)
B: No additional prophylaxis; TMP-SMX covers all required indications at this CD4 level
C: Rifampicin monotherapy for tuberculosis (no known TB exposure)
D: Itraconazole for disseminated histoplasmosis (regardless of geography)
E: Valganciclovir for CMV disease prevention
questions.explanation: MAC prophylaxis (azithromycin or clarithromycin) is indicated when CD4 count falls below 50 cells/µL — met here at 45 cells/µL. PCP and toxoplasmosis are both covered by TMP-SMX (PCP threshold <200, toxoplasmosis threshold <100). Histoplasmosis prophylaxis with itraconazole requires both CD4 <100 AND residence in an endemic area — not universally indicated. Proposing rifampicin monotherapy for TB without exposure evidence is wrong on two counts: TB prophylaxis requires a positive TST (>5mm) or known TB contact, and monotherapy is not the recommended regimen. Claiming TMP-SMX alone covers every indication at this CD4 level is also wrong, since MAC prophylaxis is not covered by TMP-SMX.
questions.topic: HIV CD4 50 MAC azithromycin clarithromycin prophylaxis thresholds

questions.question: A 29-year-old HIV-positive man has a CD4 count of 110 cells/µL. He is not in a histoplasmosis-endemic area, has no known TB exposure, and is currently on no prophylaxis. Which prophylaxis regimen is most appropriate at this CD4 level?
options: A: No prophylaxis; the threshold for any intervention is CD4 <100 cells/µL
B: TMP-SMX (PCP) plus azithromycin (MAC), as CD4 is below 200
C: TMP-SMX for both PCP and toxoplasmosis prophylaxis, since both thresholds are already met at CD4 110
D: TMP-SMX for PCP prophylaxis only — the toxoplasmosis threshold (CD4 <100) is not yet met
E: Itraconazole for histoplasmosis plus TMP-SMX for PCP
questions.explanation: At CD4 110 cells/µL: PCP prophylaxis is indicated (threshold <200 — met) but toxoplasmosis prophylaxis is NOT yet indicated (threshold <100 — not met at 110). TMP-SMX for PCP alone is correct. Adding toxoplasmosis coverage is the critical near-miss — TMP-SMX does treat toxoplasmosis too, but the indication only arises when CD4 drops below 100; at CD4 110, initiating it for toxoplasmosis is premature. MAC prophylaxis requires CD4 <50. Histoplasmosis requires CD4 <100 AND endemic area exposure.
questions.topic: HIV CD4 110 PCP TMP-SMX toxoplasmosis threshold not met prophylaxis

questions.question: A 48-year-old CMV-seropositive patient (R+) receives a kidney from a CMV-seronegative donor (D-). The transplant team discusses CMV management strategies. Which description most accurately defines the 'preemptive strategy' for CMV prophylaxis?
options: A: Antiviral therapy is reserved for patients who develop symptoms and signs of CMV disease
B: Antiviral prophylaxis is administered only to donor-seropositive/recipient-seronegative (D+/R-) transplants
C: All CMV-seropositive or otherwise high-risk post-transplant patients are started on antiviral therapy regardless of their measured viral load at any point
D: The preemptive strategy exclusively and always uses oral valganciclovir, while universal prophylaxis exclusively and always uses IV ganciclovir only
E: CMV viral load is monitored regularly and antiviral therapy is initiated at a pre-defined threshold, before clinical disease develops
questions.explanation: The preemptive strategy monitors CMV viral load and initiates antiviral therapy when a critical threshold is reached but before clinical symptoms develop. Starting all high-risk patients on antivirals irrespective of viral load instead describes universal prophylaxis, the alternative strategy. Reserving therapy for patients who already have symptoms describes reactive treatment, not prophylaxis at all. The choice of oral vs IV agent is independent of the strategy — both strategies can use either valganciclovir (oral) or ganciclovir (IV), so tying a specific drug route to a specific strategy is incorrect. The distinction between these two strategies is a high-yield EDIC topic.
questions.topic: CMV prophylaxis post-transplant preemptive universal valganciclovir ganciclovir strategy

questions.question: A 44-year-old patient undergoes allogeneic HSCT for AML. His post-transplant course is uncomplicated and he is not requiring ongoing immunosuppressive therapy. What is the recommended minimum duration of Pneumocystis jiroveci pneumonia (PJP) prophylaxis with TMP-SMX?
options: A: Lifelong prophylaxis regardless of immunosuppression status
B: 6 months post-HSCT
C: 18 months post-HSCT
D: 12 months post-HSCT, as for solid organ transplant recipients
E: 3 months post-HSCT
questions.explanation: HSCT patients receive PJP prophylaxis for 6 months; longer courses are given only if the patient remains on immunosuppressive therapy. Solid organ transplant recipients typically continue for approximately 12 months (often longer for lung transplant recipients) — proposing the solid-organ duration for this HSCT patient is the critical near-miss that tests the HSCT vs solid organ distinction. Lifelong prophylaxis is not warranted for HSCT patients once off immunosuppression.
questions.topic: PJP prophylaxis HSCT 6 months solid organ 12 months duration

questions.question: A haematology registrar asks about antibacterial prophylaxis during the pre-engraftment period (days 0–100) following allogeneic HSCT. Which statement most accurately describes fluoroquinolone prophylaxis in this setting?
options: A: Fluoroquinolones reduce both Gram-negative bacterial infection rates and overall mortality in neutropenic HSCT patients
B: Fluoroquinolones are standard of care with no controversy at all, as no resistance concerns have ever been demonstrated in HSCT populations
C: Fluoroquinolones are primarily used to prevent Gram-positive bloodstream infections in the pre-engraftment phase
D: Fluoroquinolone prophylaxis is contraindicated in all HSCT patients due to QTc prolongation risk
E: Fluoroquinolones reduce the risk of Gram-negative bacterial infection but confer no mortality benefit, and their use remains controversial
questions.explanation: Fluoroquinolone prophylaxis in the pre-engraftment period reduces the risk of Gram-negative bacterial infection in neutropenic HSCT patients; however, there is no demonstrated mortality benefit. Their use remains controversial due to concerns about toxicity and selection of resistant organisms. Adding a mortality benefit to this description overstates the evidence. Framing fluoroquinolones as primarily preventing Gram-positive bloodstream infection is incorrect — they target Gram-negative organisms, which is their primary role in this setting. Claiming there is no controversy and no resistance concern is also incorrect, since resistance selection is explicitly a recognised concern.
questions.topic: fluoroquinolone HSCT pre-engraftment Gram-negative prophylaxis no mortality benefit

questions.question: A 61-year-old with colorectal cancer has a tunneled central venous catheter for chemotherapy and has suffered three episodes of catheter-related bloodstream infection this year. Alternative venous access is not feasible. The catheter must remain in situ. Which additional measure has the strongest evidence for reducing CLABSI in this situation?
options: A: Daily systemic fluconazole to prevent fungal catheter infection
B: Prophylactic daily vancomycin infusion through the catheter
C: Antimicrobial lock solution instilled into the catheter lumen between uses
D: Applying povidone-iodine ointment to the external catheter hub only
E: Changing the catheter over a guidewire on a fixed prophylactic schedule every 4 weeks
questions.explanation: Antimicrobial lock solutions (antibiotic or antiseptic solutions instilled and 'locked' in the catheter lumen between uses) have demonstrated efficacy in reducing CLABSI and are specifically recommended when catheter removal is not feasible in cancer patients requiring long-term venous access. Antibiotic-coated catheters and antimicrobial locks are explicitly identified for immunocompromised cancer patients. Routine prophylactic vancomycin infusions are not indicated and promote resistance. Guidewire exchange does not reduce infection risk and is not a standard CLABSI prevention strategy.
questions.topic: CLABSI cancer long-term CVC antimicrobial lock solution prevention

questions.question: An immunocompromised ICU patient with multi-organ dysfunction requires nutrition support. Compared with parenteral nutrition (PN), which of the following most specifically explains why enteral nutrition (EN) is preferred in terms of infection risk?
options: A: EN avoids central venous catheter placement and thereby eliminates CLABSI risk
B: EN is always the safer choice in haemodynamically unstable patients receiving high-dose vasopressor support
C: EN prevents systemic hyperglycaemia more effectively than PN
D: EN preserves gut mucosal integrity and immune function, reducing the risk of nosocomial infection
E: EN delivers higher caloric loads during the acute phase of illness
questions.explanation: The key mechanistic advantage of enteral nutrition over parenteral nutrition is preservation of gut mucosal integrity and immune function, which reduces nosocomial infection risk. Enteral nutrition is also associated with fewer infectious and GI complications overall. Framing the benefit as simply eliminating CLABSI risk by avoiding central access describes a practical benefit, not the underlying physiological mechanism. Claiming EN is always safer in patients on high-dose vasopressors is incorrect — EN in this setting carries a risk of gut ischaemia (highlighted by NUTRIREA-2/3 data) and requires caution. Claiming EN delivers higher caloric loads is also incorrect; caloric delivery is often lower with EN, not higher.
questions.topic: enteral nutrition gut mucosal integrity immune function nosocomial infection

questions.question: A 26-year-old is admitted to the ICU following allogeneic HSCT for aplastic anaemia and is currently in the pre-engraftment phase. The dietitian asks about dietary restrictions. Which food category is specifically contraindicated to reduce the risk of foodborne infection in this period?
options: A: Raw or undercooked meals, fresh fruits and vegetables, and unpasteurised milk
B: Cooked meats and pasteurised dairy products
C: High-fibre foods and wholegrains only
D: Foods containing more than 30% fat by caloric content
E: All enteral formulas; total parenteral nutrition is mandated in the pre-engraftment period
questions.explanation: HSCT patients in the pre-engraftment period are advised to avoid raw or undercooked meals, fresh fruits and vegetables, and unpasteurised milk to reduce the risk of acquiring foodborne infections during the period of profound neutropenia. Cooked foods and pasteurised dairy are safe. TPN is not mandated in the pre-engraftment phase — enteral nutrition is preferred whenever tolerated. High-fat content or fibre level are not the primary dietary restriction concerns in this setting.
questions.topic: HSCT pre-engraftment dietary restriction raw food unpasteurised milk

questions.question: The WHO SAVE LIVES Clean Your Hands initiative identifies 5 specific moments at which hand hygiene must be performed. Which of the following is NOT one of the 5 WHO-designated moments?
options: A: After touching a patient
B: Before touching a patient
C: After body fluid exposure or risk
D: After removing personal protective equipment (PPE) following patient contact
E: After touching patient surroundings, near the end of a period of direct patient care
questions.explanation: The 5 WHO SAVE LIVES moments are: (1) before touching a patient, (2) before clean/aseptic procedures, (3) after body fluid exposure/risk, (4) after touching a patient, (5) after touching patient surroundings. 'After removing PPE' is a common and sensible practice but is NOT one of the 5 official WHO moments. This is a plausible distractor because PPE removal is taught alongside hand hygiene in infection control training, but the WHO moments framework does not include it explicitly.
questions.topic: WHO SAVE LIVES 5 moments hand hygiene NOT PPE removal

questions.question: Regarding hand hygiene in the ICU:

statements.label: A
statements.text: The WHO SAVE LIVES initiative includes 'after touching patient surroundings' as one of the 5 defined moments for hand hygiene.

statements.label: B
statements.text: A combination of chlorhexidine and alcohol covers Gram-positive bacteria, Gram-negative bacteria, viruses, mycobacteria, and fungi.

statements.label: C
statements.text: Alcohol-based hand rubs are effective against C. difficile and other spore-forming organisms.

statements.label: D
statements.text: Video surveillance studies show that healthcare workers demonstrate better hand hygiene compliance than patients' relatives.

questions.explanation: Claiming alcohol-based preparations are effective against C. difficile and other spore-forming organisms is false: they do not remove or inactivate spores, and soap and water is required instead. The chlorhexidine-alcohol combination covering the broad spectrum listed is correct, as is 'after touching patient surroundings' being the fifth WHO SAVE LIVES moment. Claiming healthcare workers demonstrate better compliance than patients' relatives is false: a Japanese ICU video surveillance study showed the opposite — relatives were actually better than healthcare workers at adhering to hand hygiene policy.
questions.topic: hand hygiene alcohol spores CHX WHO moments compliance workers

questions.question: Regarding isolation requirements for immunocompromised patients in the ICU:

statements.label: A
statements.text: Contact isolation with gloves and gown alone is sufficient to prevent Aspergillus transmission in HSCT patients, without the need for HEPA-filtered rooms.

statements.label: B
statements.text: Neutropenic patients who have not undergone HSCT generally do not require placement in isolation rooms.

statements.label: C
statements.text: HEPA filtration in the HSCT patient's room helps reduce the risk of invasive Aspergillosis.

statements.label: D
statements.text: HSCT patients should be placed in single well-sealed rooms with more than 12 air exchanges per hour.

questions.explanation: Non-HSCT neutropenic patients not requiring isolation rooms, HSCT patients requiring sealed single rooms with >12 air changes/hour and HEPA filtration, and HEPA filtration reducing inhaled Aspergillus spore burden are all correctly described. Claiming contact isolation with gloves and gown alone is sufficient to prevent Aspergillus transmission is false: Aspergillus is an airborne environmental mould requiring engineered air filtration, not just PPE-based contact precautions, which instead target contact-transmitted pathogens such as MRSA.
questions.topic: HSCT isolation HEPA air exchanges Aspergillosis neutropenia no isolation room

questions.question: Regarding prevention of ventilator-associated pneumonia (VAP) in mechanically ventilated ICU patients:

statements.label: A
statements.text: Prophylactic systemic antifungal therapy is a routine component of all standard VAP prevention bundles.

statements.label: B
statements.text: Selective digestive decontamination (SDD) has been shown to increase mortality in mechanically ventilated ICU patients.

statements.label: C
statements.text: Head-of-bed elevation to 45 degrees is a standard VAP prevention measure.

statements.label: D
statements.text: Subglottic suctioning endotracheal tubes are a recognised component of VAP prevention bundles.

questions.explanation: Subglottic suctioning ETTs and 45° head-of-bed elevation being well-established VAP bundle components is correct. Claiming SDD increases mortality is false: SDD has been shown to modestly reduce ICU mortality, not increase it, as confirmed by systematic review and meta-analysis. Claiming prophylactic systemic antifungal therapy is a routine bundle component is also false: it is reserved for specific immunocompromised populations based on underlying disease, not universal VAP prevention.
questions.topic: VAP bundle subglottic head elevation SDD mortality antifungal NOT standard

questions.question: Regarding selective decontamination strategies in the ICU:

statements.label: A
statements.text: Both SOD and SDD have been shown to reduce the risk of ICU-acquired respiratory tract infections.

statements.label: B
statements.text: Longitudinal follow-up data consistently demonstrate a significant increase in multidrug-resistant organism carriage in ICUs that have adopted SDD.

statements.label: C
statements.text: Selective oropharyngeal decontamination (SOD) consists of applying topical non-absorbable antimicrobials to the oropharynx only, without systemic antibiotics.

statements.label: D
statements.text: Selective digestive decontamination (SDD) includes both oral non-absorbable antimicrobials and a short course of systemic antibiotics.

questions.explanation: SOD as topical non-absorbable antimicrobials to the oropharynx only, and SDD adding a short-course systemic antibiotic to that topical regimen, are both correctly distinguished, as is both strategies reducing ICU-acquired respiratory tract infection risk. Claiming longitudinal data consistently show significantly increased MDRO carriage with SDD adoption is false: this is the common theoretical concern, but current evidence points to the contrary — longitudinal follow-up has not demonstrated increased resistant organisms in adopting ICUs.
questions.topic: SDD SOD differences resistance evidence VAP reduction mortality

questions.question: Regarding antimicrobial prophylaxis in HIV-infected patients based on CD4 count:

statements.label: A
statements.text: Tuberculosis prophylaxis with isoniazid is indicated at any CD4 level if the tuberculin skin test exceeds 5mm or if there is known contact with an active TB case.

statements.label: B
statements.text: TMP-SMX for Pneumocystis jiroveci (PCP) prophylaxis is indicated when CD4 count falls below 200 cells/µL.

statements.label: C
statements.text: Mycobacterium avium complex (MAC) prophylaxis with azithromycin or clarithromycin is indicated when CD4 count falls below 100 cells/µL.

statements.label: D
statements.text: Toxoplasmosis prophylaxis is indicated when CD4 count falls below 100 cells/µL.

questions.explanation: PCP prophylaxis threshold of CD4 <200, toxoplasmosis threshold of CD4 <100, and TB prophylaxis being based on TST result or known contact rather than a specific CD4 threshold are all correct. Claiming MAC prophylaxis is indicated at CD4 <100 is false and the classic trap: the actual MAC threshold is CD4 <50 cells/µL, not <100 — confusing the MAC and toxoplasmosis thresholds is a common error.
questions.topic: HIV prophylaxis CD4 thresholds PCP Toxo MAC 50 TB TST

questions.question: Regarding CMV prophylaxis following solid organ transplantation:

statements.label: A
statements.text: Valganciclovir or ganciclovir is the first-line agent for CMV prophylaxis in post-transplant patients.

statements.label: B
statements.text: CMV-seropositive solid organ transplant recipients typically receive CMV prophylaxis for three to six months.

statements.label: C
statements.text: Universal CMV prophylaxis involves monitoring viral load and initiating antiviral therapy when a pre-defined threshold is reached.

statements.label: D
statements.text: Prophylaxis against HSV and VZV with acyclovir is recommended for all seropositive post-transplant patients not already receiving CMV prophylaxis.

questions.explanation: Valganciclovir/ganciclovir as first-line CMV prophylactic agents, and the 3-6 month standard duration for CMV-seropositive solid organ recipients, are correct, as is acyclovir being recommended for HSV/VZV-seropositive patients not already on CMV prophylaxis. Describing universal prophylaxis as monitoring viral load and treating at a threshold is false and the trap here: that description instead applies to the PREEMPTIVE strategy — universal prophylaxis starts all high-risk patients on antivirals regardless of viral load.
questions.topic: CMV prophylaxis universal preemptive valganciclovir ganciclovir solid organ VZV HSV

questions.question: Regarding PJP prophylaxis following transplantation:

statements.label: A
statements.text: Solid organ transplant recipients receive the same duration of PJP prophylaxis as HSCT patients (6 months).

statements.label: B
statements.text: HSCT patients not requiring ongoing immunosuppressive therapy typically receive PJP prophylaxis for 6 months.

statements.label: C
statements.text: TMP-SMX is the first-line agent for PJP prophylaxis in all transplant recipients.

statements.label: D
statements.text: Lung transplant recipients often require PJP prophylaxis for longer than 12 months.

questions.explanation: TMP-SMX as first-line PJP prophylaxis (with dapsone, atovaquone, or pentamidine as alternatives when not tolerated), HSCT patients off immunosuppression typically receiving 6 months of prophylaxis, and lung transplant recipients often requiring more than 12 months are all correct. Claiming solid organ recipients receive the same 6-month duration as HSCT patients is false: solid organ recipients typically receive approximately 12 months — the key discriminating duration between HSCT and solid organ transplantation.
questions.topic: PJP duration HSCT 6 months solid organ lung transplant

questions.question: Regarding routine microbiological surveillance in the ICU:

statements.label: A
statements.text: A recognised concern about MDRO isolation rooms is a potential decrease in quality of care due to reduced healthcare worker time with isolated patients.

statements.label: B
statements.text: The cost-effectiveness of routine microbiological ICU surveillance is well-established through large randomised controlled trials.

statements.label: C
statements.text: A positive MDRO surveillance culture in an ICU patient indicates active infection requiring immediate antibiotic treatment.

statements.label: D
statements.text: Adding targeted decolonisation to active surveillance and barrier precautions may further reduce MDRO transmission.

questions.explanation: Claiming a positive MDRO surveillance culture indicates active infection requiring immediate treatment is false: surveillance cultures detect colonisation, and the presence of an MDRO does not necessarily indicate active infection requiring treatment. Adding targeted decolonisation to surveillance and barrier precautions further reducing MDRO transmission is correct, as is reduced healthcare worker time with isolated patients being a recognised concern about care quality. Claiming the cost-effectiveness of routine surveillance is well-established through large RCTs is also false: evidence remains mixed, and the field lacks robust randomised data for this specific intervention.
questions.section: Routine Microbiological Surveillance in the ICU
questions.topic: MDRO surveillance colonisation targeted decolonisation isolation quality care

questions.question: Regarding enteral nutrition (EN) in critically ill ICU patients:

statements.label: A
statements.text: Enteral nutrition preserves gut mucosal integrity and immune function, thereby reducing the risk of nosocomial infection.

statements.label: B
statements.text: Enteral nutrition should be preferred over parenteral nutrition whenever clinically feasible.

statements.label: C
statements.text: Enteral nutrition is associated with fewer infectious complications compared with parenteral nutrition.

statements.label: D
statements.text: Initiating full-calorie enteral feeding within 24 hours of ICU admission consistently demonstrates mortality benefit without risk of feeding intolerance.

questions.explanation: Enteral nutrition reducing infectious complications compared with parenteral nutrition, and preserving gut mucosal integrity and immune function, are both correct, as is EN being preferred over PN whenever clinically feasible. Claiming full-calorie feeding within 24 hours consistently shows a mortality benefit without risk of intolerance is false: while early feeding within 24-48h is recommended, claims of a consistent mortality benefit from full-calorie early feeding are not well supported — concerns of bias exist in early studies, and permissive underfeeding is well tolerated and avoids feeding intolerance, as highlighted by the NUTRIREA-2/3 trials' findings on the risks of full-rate feeding in haemodynamically unstable patients.
questions.topic: enteral nutrition gut mucosa EN vs PN early feeding permissive

questions.question: Regarding the use of probiotics in critically ill immunocompromised patients:

statements.label: A
statements.text: Some studies have demonstrated a reduction in post-operative infections following liver transplantation with probiotic use.

statements.label: B
statements.text: Probiotics have modest evidence for a role in preventing Clostridium difficile-associated diarrhoea (CDAD).

statements.label: C
statements.text: Probiotics are considered safe and are routinely recommended in all immunocompromised ICU patients.

statements.label: D
statements.text: Evidence conclusively supports routine probiotic use for prevention of nosocomial pneumonia in all critically ill patients.

questions.explanation: Limited evidence supporting a modest role for probiotics in CDAD prevention, and some studies showing reduced post-operative infection specifically after liver transplantation, are both correct — one of the few immunocompromised populations with favourable probiotic data. Claiming evidence conclusively supports routine probiotic use for nosocomial pneumonia prevention in all critically ill patients is false: the evidence is mixed and the role remains unclear, not conclusive. Claiming probiotics are safe and routinely recommended in all immunocompromised ICU patients is also false: safety concerns exist in this population (risk of translocation/sepsis with live organisms), making routine recommendation inappropriate.
questions.topic: probiotics CDAD C difficile liver transplant immunocompromised safety controversial
